food_name: Bubur Kacang (Mung Bean Dessert Soup with Coconut Milk and Palm Sugar)

local_names.romanized_local: Lǜ Dòu Tāng (Chinese); Bubur Kacang Hijau (Malay); Paccaip Payaṟu Kaṇci (Tamil)

food_subgroup: Legume-based dessert soup; mung beans simmered with coconut milk and palm sugar (gula melaka)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has mung bean (FOODON_03309375) but does not have a specific term for the Southeast Asian dessert soup preparation with coconut milk and palm sugar. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Legume-based sweet dish — closest FoodEx2 parent for mung bean dessert soup; no exact FoodEx2 term exists for this preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for bubur kacang / mung bean dessert soup. A00GG (legume-based dishes) used as closest defensible parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of bubur kacang is approximately 250 ml (250 g), as commonly served at hawker centres and kuih stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bubur Kacang Hijau — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central (Mung beans, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt; Coconut milk, raw)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and primary micronutrients sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for bubur kacang hijau and IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Trace minerals (copper, manganese) and vitamin K supplemented from USDA FoodData Central composite of cooked mung beans and coconut milk, consistent with the input record's enriched_json provenance flags. All values cross-checked against the input verified record (confidence 1.0 for primary macros).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — mung bean / legume dessert soup analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bubur kacang hijau as a composite dish. GI estimated at ~50 based on: (1) cooked whole mung beans GI ~25–31 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021); (2) palm sugar (gula melaka) GI ~35 (lower than white sugar GI ~65); (3) coconut milk has negligible GI contribution. Composite GI of ~50 is a conservative upward adjustment from the mung bean base to account for the added sugars and partial breakdown of beans during prolonged simmering. GL per 250 g serving = (50 × 13.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 17.25, rounded to 17.3. This estimate is consistent with published analogous legume dessert GI data.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (30 mg/100 g; 75 mg per 250 g bowl) with beneficial potassium content (120 mg/100 g; 300 mg per bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Bubur kacang is an excellent dessert choice for hypertensive patients: sodium content is very low at 75 mg per bowl, and the potassium-to-sodium ratio is favourable (4:1), supporting blood pressure regulation; patients should avoid versions with added salt or salted accompaniments such as cakoi.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~50) and moderate glycemic load (~17.3 per 250 g bowl) driven by high-fibre, high-protein mung bean base and low-GI palm sugar (gula melaka GI ~35)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bubur kacang is the most clinically appropriate traditional Singaporean dessert for patients with Type 2 Diabetes: the mung bean base (GI ~25–31 whole cooked) combined with gula melaka sweetening yields a composite GI of ~50 and a GL of ~17 per bowl, and the 5.25 g dietary fibre per serving further attenuates postprandial glucose response; it may be consumed as an occasional dessert within a carbohydrate-controlled meal plan, with portion limited to one standard bowl.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, moderate saturated fat (2.2 g/100 g; 5.5 g per bowl from coconut milk MCTs), high dietary fibre, and anti-inflammatory mung bean flavonoids (vitexin, isovitexin)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bubur kacang is suitable for patients with cardiovascular disease as an occasional dessert: it contains zero cholesterol, moderate saturated fat (5.5 g per bowl, predominantly medium-chain triglycerides from coconut milk which have a more neutral cardiovascular profile than long-chain saturated fats), and the mung bean flavonoids vitexin and isovitexin have demonstrated NF-κB inhibitory activity in cell studies; total fat per bowl is 6.75 g, which is low for a dessert.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory mung bean polyphenols; high folate (45 mcg per bowl) relevant for methotrexate users; magnesium 45 mg per bowl
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bubur kacang is suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis: mung beans contain vitexin and isovitexin with anti-inflammatory properties, folate content (18 mcg/100 g; 45 mcg per bowl) is clinically relevant for patients on methotrexate therapy, and zero cholesterol with moderate MCT saturated fat supports an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bubur Kacang (also known as Bubur Kacang Hijau in Malay, 绿豆汤 Lǜ Dòu Tāng in Chinese, and பச்சைப் பயறு கஞ்சி in Tamil) is a beloved traditional dessert soup consumed across all ethnic communities in Singapore and Malaysia. It is prepared by boiling whole mung beans (Vigna radiata) until they soften and burst, then simmering them with rich coconut milk, palm sugar (gula melaka), fragrant pandan leaves, and a pinch of salt. The result is a warm, creamy, mildly sweet soup with a soft, slightly grainy texture from the burst beans and a distinctive caramel-like depth from the gula melaka. It is served warm at hawker centres, Malay kuih stalls, Chinese dessert shops, and Peranakan restaurants, typically as a tea-time snack or breakfast dish. Nutritionally, bubur kacang is the most clinically appropriate traditional Singaporean dessert for patients with Type 2 Diabetes: the mung bean base has one of the lowest GI values of any food (GI ~25–31 whole cooked), and the composite dish GI is approximately 50 with a glycemic load of ~17.3 per 250 g bowl. Dietary fibre is 5.25 g per bowl, slowing carbohydrate absorption. Sodium is very low at 75 mg per bowl. The dish is naturally vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Key nutrients per 250 g bowl: 215 kcal, 5.25 g protein, 6.75 g fat (5.5 g saturated, predominantly MCTs from coconut milk), 34.5 g carbohydrate, 5.25 g dietary fibre, 300 mg potassium, 45 mcg folate, and zero cholesterol.
common_user_queries: is bubur kacang good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database and IMR Malaysia MyFCD with confidence 1.0 per input record. Trace minerals (copper, manganese) and vitamin K supplemented from USDA FoodData Central composite with confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked mung beans and palm sugar, clearly documented. All values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish composition.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for bubur kacang hijau as a composite dish is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB/IMR source — plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — entirely plant-based ingredients; coconut milk contains no cholesterol). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no beta-carotene-rich ingredients; pandan leaves contribute negligible RAE at typical usage levels). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — entirely plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin D sources in this plant-based preparation). Copper (0.12 mg/100 g), manganese (0.25 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (2.5 mcg/100 g) values sourced from USDA FoodData Central composite of cooked mung beans (FDC ID 175238) and coconut milk (FDC ID 169411), consistent with input enriched_json provenance. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for bubur kacang hijau as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Two default portions exist in source data: 250 g (primary, HPB-verified) and 200 g (secondary, enriched_json); 250 g used as canonical serving size. FoodEx2 code A00GG used as closest defensible parent for legume-based sweet dish; no exact FoodEx2 term exists for this preparation.
